journal-meta.journal-id: Int J Cardiol Cardiovasc Risk Prev
Int J Cardiol Cardiovasc Risk Prev

journal-title-group.journal-title: International Journal of Cardiology. Cardiovascular Risk and Prevention

title-group.article-title: Comment on “Dose-dependent effects of SGLT2 inhibitors on circadian blood pressure in hypertensive patients with diabetes: A systematic review and Bayesian network meta-analysis”

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Cardiology, The Central Hospital of Yongzhou, Yongzhou, China
bDepartment of Cardiology, Yongzhou Hospital Affiliated to University of South China, Yongzhou, China

author-notes.corresp: ⁎Corresponding author. Department of Cardiology, Yongzhou Hospital Affiliated to University of South China, Yiyun Street NO.396, Yongzhou, 425000, Hunan Province, China. caobinyzs@163.com

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

body.p: We read with interest the meta-analysis by Davood et al. [1], which concluded that SGLT2 inhibitors (SGLT2is) modestly reduce blood pressure (BP) without clear dose-dependency. However, we note methodological limitations that warrant discussion.

First, categorizing dosages as "low, medium, high" across different SGLT2is oversimplifies dose-response relationships. Pharmacokinetic and potency differences exist. Sato et al. found that without standardization (e.g., by urinary glucose excretion), potency differences of 2.3-fold can occur; after standardization, canagliflozin still showed 1.33-fold higher intrinsic activity [2]. Future analyses should use pharmacologically-driven dose modeling.

Second, the validity of indirect comparisons in the star-shaped network relies on transitivity. Chen et al. demonstrated that key modifiers like diabetes duration (>15 years: HR 0.78; <5 years: HR 0.65; P = 0.003) and baseline hypertension severity substantially alter treatment effects, threatening indirect comparison validity [3]. The conclusion of comparable efficacy may thus be unreliable without assessing these factors.

Third, the "non-dose-dependent" conclusion requires scrutiny. The "effect ceiling" phenomenon may explain the lack of gradient. More critically, original trials were underpowered for BP endpoints. Georgianos et al. confirmed sample sizes were based on glycemic outcomes, leading to severe underpowering for detecting BP differences. With sample sizes <200/group, the false-negative rate for dose-response reached 47% [4]. Interpreting "no significant effect" as "no dose-dependency" may be biased.
Future studies with refined dose models, rigorous transitivity checks, and BP-focused designs are needed. We believe addressing these points will strengthen future evidence synthesis.

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: Yin Jian: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Bin Cao: Conceptualization, Supervision, Visualization.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: The authors declare that no funds, grants, or other support were received during the preparation of this manuscript.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have influenced the work reported in this paper.

element-citation.article-title: Dose-dependent effects of SGLT2 inhibitors on circadian blood pressure in hypertensive patients with diabetes: a systematic review and bayesian network meta-analysis
